name: Burkina Faso
summary: Burkina Faso is a West African Sahel civil-law unitary republic whose family-law framework operates under the Persons and Family Code 1989 (Code des Personnes et de la Famille, Decree 89/PRES/SASA-RM) drawing on French civil-law substantive heritage with substantial codification of customary-law and Islamic-law marriage provisions for relevant communities. Parental authority and child custody are governed by Persons and Family Code arts. 510-565. The Court of Cassation (Cour de Cassation) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Council (Conseil Constitutionnel) operates separate constitutional review. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Court of First Instance (Tribunal de Grande Instance). Psychology profession is regulated through the Ministry of Health framework. Burkina Faso is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the interest-of-the-child standard. Burkina Faso acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 May 1992 — earliest African Hague accession in the corpus.

statutory_framework.title: Persons and Family Code — Parental authority and custody
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Persons and Family Code with codification of customary-law and Islamic-law marriage provisions. Arts. 510-565 govern parental authority and child custody.

apex_courts.name: Court of Cassation (Cour de Cassation)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Council (Conseil Constitutionnel)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Council with original jurisdiction over constitutional review.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Health and Public Hygiene, Burkina Faso
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology.

anonymisation_convention: Burkinabé family-court decisions are anonymised per Court of Cassation practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Upper Volta independence + renamed Burkina Faso 1984
key_developments.description: Upper Volta (Haute-Volta) achieved independence 5 August 1960 from France. Pre-1960 French civil-law framework substantively retained as foundational legal-system substrate. Country renamed Burkina Faso ('Land of Honest People') 4 August 1984 under Thomas Sankara revolutionary government (1983-1987).

key_developments.title: Sankara presidency + revolutionary reforms (1983-1987)
key_developments.description: Thomas Sankara assumed presidency 4 August 1983 — substantively distinctive revolutionary-Marxist-Pan-African presidency including women's-rights reforms, land redistribution, anti-colonial policies. Assassinated 15 October 1987 by Blaise Compaoré coup. Sankara reforms substantively affected family-law reform pipeline that resulted in 1989 Persons and Family Code.

key_developments.title: Persons and Family Code 1989 + post-Sankara codification
key_developments.description: Federal Persons and Family Code enacted 1989 under Compaoré government with codification of customary-law and Islamic-law marriage provisions for relevant communities. Arts. 510-565 govern parental authority and child custody. Drew substantively on Sankara-era women's-rights reform agenda including monogamy promotion and customary-law modernisation.

key_developments.title: Burkina Faso ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Burkina Faso ratified the UNCRC on 31 August 1990 — among the earliest African ratifications globally. Framing the family-law-reform trajectory toward best-interests-of-the-child substantive doctrine.

key_developments.title: Constitution 1991 + multi-party transition
key_developments.description: Constitution of the Fourth Republic adopted 2 June 1991 by referendum (substantially amended 2012) — establishing semi-presidential republic framework. Codifies family-protection-clauses and children's-rights provisions. Compaoré presidency 1987-2014.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession — earliest African
key_developments.description: Burkina Faso acceded to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction 1980 effective 1 May 1992 — earliest African Hague accession in the corpus. Structurally distinctive within African corpus as early Hague-Africa-mover.

key_developments.title: Popular Uprising + Compaoré ouster + democratic transition
key_developments.description: Popular Uprising 30-31 October 2014 ousted Blaise Compaoré after 27 years (1987-2014) — democratic transition trajectory establishing transitional civilian-military framework. Subsequent multi-party elections 2015 won by Roch Marc Christian Kaboré.

key_developments.title: Coup d'état 2022 + military junta + Traoré presidency
key_developments.description: Two coups d'état 2022 — January 2022 (Damiba) and September 2022 (Traoré). Ibrahim Traoré assumed presidency 30 September 2022 establishing military junta framework. Substantially affected international engagement including EU/Western relations. Substantive jihadist insurgency context affecting family-law-implementation in northern regions.

key_developments.title: ECOWAS withdrawal + Sahel States Alliance + regional realignment
key_developments.description: Burkina Faso (with Mali, Niger) announced ECOWAS withdrawal January 2024 (effective January 2025) and formed Sahel States Alliance (Alliance of Sahel States, AES) — substantively distinctive regional-realignment affecting cross-border family-law engagement. Substantive demographic displacement context affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Insurrection populaire framework + Sankaranist-revival institutional context + family-law continuity in Ouagadougou
key_developments.description: Ibrahim Traoré sankaranist-revival institutional framework 2023 affecting institutional context for family-law adjudication. Conseil National de la Transition framework. Burkina Faso Commission Nationale des Droits Humains continuing institutional position addressing PA-related issues within Persons and Family Code framework. Family-law continuity in Ouagadougou + non-jihadist-affected regions. Substantively significant Sahel institutional context.

key_developments.title: Court of Cassation + Constitutional Council — interest-of-the-child substantive register in constrained operational context
key_developments.description: Court of Cassation and Constitutional Council continue to develop interest-of-the-child jurisprudence under Persons and Family Code arts. 510-565 in custody disputes within constrained operational context of ongoing jihadist insurgency and military-junta governance. Substantive analysis without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption.

structural_findings: Burkina Faso operates a French-civil-law family-law framework with codified customary-law and Islamic-law marriage provisions — places Burkina Faso in the Sahel Francophone West African cluster with Mali, Niger, Senegal.
Hague Convention 1980 accession 1992 places Burkina Faso as earliest African Hague accession within the corpus.
Persons and Family Code 1989 codification of customary-law and Islamic-law marriage within civil-law framework is structurally distinctive within Sahel cluster.

sources.title: Ministry of Health and Public Hygiene

editorial_notes: Burkina Faso jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 2 to 10 key_developments with full Upper-Volta-to-Sahel-Alliance trajectory: 1960-Upper-Volta-independence-renamed-Burkina-Faso-1984 + 1984-Sankara-presidency-revolutionary-reforms + 1989-Persons-and-Family-Code-post-Sankara-codification + 1990-UNCRC-ratification + 1991-Constitution-multi-party + 1992-Hague-1980-accession-earliest-African + 2014-Popular-Uprising-Compaoré-ouster + 2022-Coup-d'état-+-Traoré-presidency + 2024-ECOWAS-withdrawal-+-Sahel-States-Alliance + 2025-Court-of-Cassation-Constitutional-Council-interest-of-the-child-constrained-context.

Civil-law Sahel Francophone West Africa (French civil-law substantive + Persons and Family Code 1989 customary-law-Islamic-law-codified + Constitution 1991 + Hague Convention 1980 accession 1992 — earliest African + post-2022-military-junta + post-2024-Sahel-States-Alliance).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive interest-of-the-child analysis under Persons and Family Code arts. 510-565 framework within constrained operational context without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins Sahel-Francophone-West-African + civil-law + customary-Islamic-law-codified-distinctive + earliest-African-Hague-Convention-1992 + Sankara-revolutionary-presidency-legacy (1983-1987) + post-2022-military-junta-governance + post-2024-Sahel-States-Alliance-with-Mali-Niger-ECOWAS-withdrawal + ongoing-jihadist-insurgency-constrained-operational-context + Traoré-youngest-African-leader (different from Faye Senegal) clusters within the corpus.
